one_line_takeaway: MoE2 turns a fleet of heterogeneous edge LLMs into a mixture-of-experts whose subset is chosen by a provably-optimal monotonic optimization under latency/energy budgets, decoupling one-time gating training from per-query top-k routing to beat single-agent and majority-voting baselines on MMLU.
key_finding: In simulation on MMLU, MoE2 achieves the highest accuracy, beating a Single Agent by 14.4%, Majority Voting by 7.3%, and Average Expert Accuracy by 16.4%. On the real testbed under resource constraints, MoE2 consistently matches or beats the SMO-Majority-Voting and Random-Subset baselines and exploits looser budgets best: at tau_max=2s it beats Random M.V. by an average of 9.4% and SMO M.V. by 1.38%; at tau_max=3s it beats Random M.V. by up to 18.0% (at 25J) and SMO M.V. by 3.2-3.3% at 35-50J. The gating network is small enough (as little as 1.7 GB) to co-locate on edge servers. The two theorems are the load-bearing result: they collapse a per-subset retraining problem into a single gating-training pass plus a monotonic-optimization search.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MoE2 (Mixture-of-Edge-Experts), a collaborative inference framework that lets a set of heterogeneous edge-deployed LLMs act as 'experts' whose token-level output distributions are fused via a gating network. Its central novelty is a two-level expert-selection mechanism plus supporting theory: (1) a coarse-grained, optimization-based selection of an expert subset S that guarantees worst-case bounds on latency and energy, and (2) a fine-grained top-k routing that picks the best experts per prompt from S. The authors formulate the joint gating-parameter-training and expert-selection problem as a constrained combinatorial optimization (min ensemble loss subject to per-application latency deadlines and an energy budget). They prove two structural results that make it tractable: Theorem 1 (optimality-preserving property) shows the optimal gating parameters for the full expert set N yield the optimal normalized weights for ANY subset S, so gating training can be decoupled from subset selection and done only once; Theorem 2 (monotonic improvement) shows adding experts never increases the optimal loss, enabling a Subset Monotonic Optimization (SMO) algorithm for provably (epsilon-)optimal subset selection. A cluster-based method (K-means over prompt embeddings, K=8) is used to synthesize domain-specialized experts by LoRA-fine-tuning base models per cluster, without manual labeling. This is the paper's OWN contribution; MoE background (sparse gating, top-k) and prior edge-LLM inference schemes are cited related work, not claimed here.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a systems/optimization paper on resource-constrained collaborative LLM inference at the network edge (arXiv cs.NI). It defines no adversary, no attack surface, and makes no confidentiality/integrity/availability claims. The 'costs' it reasons about are latency and energy budgets, not security threats. The knowledge-graph record's cluster_label 'LLM Privacy Leakage' and its abstract field (a stray, unrelated IEEE Access citation) are misclassifications; no security frame should be imposed.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to a single benchmark family (MMLU as the held-out test; MMLU-pro/ARC/SciQ/AGIEval for expert fine-tuning) and to multiple-choice QA accuracy, so generalization to open-ended generation is untested. The system is static: the authors themselves flag dynamic user demand and fluctuating server load as future work. Only N=8 experts and 8 clusters are studied; scalability of the SMO combinatorial search to larger expert pools is not empirically stressed. Theorems 1 and 2 rely on an MLP gating network 'of sufficiently large width' via universal approximation, an idealization that may not hold for the small (128-hidden-unit) MLP actually deployed. Improvements over the strong SMO-Majority-Voting baseline are modest (often 1-3%). The constraints require prompt distribution P_x, which is not directly accessible; the authors argue delay/energy depend mainly on prompt length (significant only beyond 1024 tokens), which sidesteps but does not fully solve the estimation issue. No multi-modal or non-fine-tuned agent support yet.

datasets_used: MMLU (held-out evaluation dataset; 80% used to train the gating network, 20% for testing)
MMLU-pro (used only for expert fine-tuning, not evaluation)
quantitative_results: Simulation on MMLU: MoE2 outperforms Single Agent by 14.4%, Majority Voting by 7.3%, and Average Expert Accuracy by 16.4%
Testbed tau_max=1s: MoE2 reaches up to 59.9 accuracy and improves 4.2% over Random M.V. at 50J
Testbed tau_max=2s: MoE2 peaks at 71.8 accuracy; outperforms SMO M.V. by avg 1.38% and Random M.V. by avg 9.4%
Testbed tau_max=3s: MoE2 outperforms Random M.V. by up to 18.0% (at 25J) and SMO M.V. by 3.2% and 3.3% at 35J and 50J respectively
Gating network deployable at as small as 1.7 GB
System scale: N=8 edge servers/experts, K=8 embedding clusters; delay/energy variation statistically significant only beyond 1024-token prompts
Fine-tuning: LoRA rank 256, alpha 512, 3 epochs; gating MLP input dim 1024, hidden dim 128, output dim 8
baselines_compared: Single Agent (S.A.) - each original model evaluated individually
Majority Voting (M.V.) - equal-weight fusion of all fine-tuned agents, no gating network
Average Expert Accuracy (A.E.A.) - mean per-expert accuracy
SMO with Majority Voting (SMO M.V.) - equal weights over the SMO-selected subset
Random Subset Selection with Majority Voting (Rand. M.V.) - equal weights over a randomly selected subset
